signals.idea: Split useTimelineData.ts (678 LOC) — the last god hook in the video editor. It mixes 6 concerns: (1) Data fetching/queries (timelineQuery, assetRegistryQuery, saveMutation) ~60 lines. (2) Save/sync lifecycle (saveTimeline, scheduleSave, commitData, applyTimelineEdit, applyResolvedConfigEdit, patchRegistry, editSeqRef, savedSeqRef, saveTimer, lastSavedSignature) ~200 lines. (3) Render state (renderStatus, renderProgress, renderLog, renderDirty, renderResult*, useClientRender wiring) ~60 lines. (4) UI preferences (scaleWidth, activeClipTab, assetPanel state, setters) ~50 lines. (5) Derived/memoized data (resolvedConfig, selectedClip, selectedTrack, compositionSize, renderMetadata, trackScaleMap) ~100 lines. (6) Asset operations (uploadAsset, registerAsset, uploadFiles, invalidateAssetRegistry, resolveAssetUrl, reloadFromServer) ~50 lines. Goal: each concern in its own hook under 200 LOC, useTimelineData becomes a thin orchestrator under 150 LOC that composes them. No behavioral changes — pure extraction.

unresolved_flags.concern: Over-engineering: the `useTimelineSave` size target is not realistic for this repository as written. Step 5 still bundles almost all of the heavy logic from `useTimelineData` into one hook, so the executor will likely either miss the `<200 LOC` success criterion or introduce extra helper files that contradict the "6 extracted hooks only" constraint.

resolved_flags.concern: Step 3 is incomplete: `useDerivedTimeline` cannot be a straight move of the `useMemo` blocks, because `resolvedConfig` currently depends on `configSignatureRef` and `stableResolvedConfigRef` to preserve object identity across poll churn. If that stabilization is dropped or left behind in the orchestrator, the split stops being a pure extraction and risks preview remount/regression.
resolved_flags.resolution:
Three targeted revisions addressing all gate flags:

1. **`configSignatureRef`/`stableResolvedConfigRef` → `useDerivedTimeline`** (Step 3): Explicitly moved these refs into the derived hook alongside the `resolvedConfig` memo. RemotionPreview depends on referential stability of `config.clips`; the stabilization refs must travel with the memo.

2. **`setRenderDirty` wiring via `onSaveSuccessRef`** (Steps 5, 7): The orchestrator creates a `useRef` for `onSaveSuccessRef`, passes it to `useTimelineSave`, and populates it with `() => render.setRenderDirty(true)` after both hooks are constructed. `saveTimeline`'s `onSuccess` calls `onSaveSuccessRef.current?.()` instead of `setRenderDirty(true)` directly. This preserves the save→render-dirty signal without coupling the hooks.

3. **`reloadFromServer` → `useTimelineSave`** (Steps 5, 6): Moved from assumption of living in `useAssetOperations` to living in `useTimelineSave`. The function resets editSeqRef/savedSeqRef, calls commitData, and sets saveStatus — all save/sync internals. The asset hook is now thinner (~40 LOC) and has no dependency on save internals.

Also revised the orchestrator call order (Step 7) to: prefs → queries → save → derived → render → assets, with explicit explanation of why this order works with the ref-based wiring.

resolved_flags.concern: The plan does not preserve the dependency from save/sync to render state. `renderDirty` is not just render-local UI state; it is flipped to `true` when a save succeeds. Extracting `useRenderState` and `useTimelineSave` without explicitly threading that setter will change behavior.
resolved_flags.resolution:

2. **`setRenderDirty` wiring via `onSaveSuccessRef`** (Steps 5, 7): The orchestrator creates a `useRef` for `onSaveSuccessRef`, passes it to `useTimelineSave`, and populates it with `() => render.setRenderDirty(true)` after both hooks are constructed. `saveTimeline`'s `onSuccess` calls `onSaveSuccessRef.current?.()` instead of `setRenderDirty(true)` directly. The function resets editSeqRef/savedSeqRef, calls commitData, and sets saveStatus — all save/sync internals. The asset hook is now thinner (~40 LOC) and has no dependency on save internals.

Also revised the orchestrator call order (Step 7) to: prefs → queries → save → derived → render → assets, with explicit explanation of why this order works with the ref-based wiring.

resolved_flags.concern: Keeping `reloadFromServer` in `useAssetOperations` is the wrong boundary for this repository. That function is a save/sync reset path, not an asset helper, and moving it there forces the asset hook to depend on save internals anyway. This undermines the stated goal of concern isolation.

signals.loop_summary: Iteration 2. Weighted score trajectory: 4.75 -> 0.5. Plan deltas: 51.3%. Recurring critiques: 0. Resolved flags: 3. Open significant flags: 1.

items: Each extracted hook is under 200 LOC
No new files beyond the 6 extracted hooks (no unnecessary abstractions)
npx tsc --noEmit passes with zero errors after all changes
The UseTimelineDataResult interface is unchanged (identical public API)
All existing imports from useTimelineData resolve correctly (ClipTab, SaveStatus, RenderStatus, UseTimelineDataResult, shouldAcceptPolledData)
useTimelineState.types.ts compiles with ReturnType<typeof useTimelineData> unchanged
Video editor loads, edits, saves, and renders identically (manual verification)
Remotion preview does not remount on edit (referential stability preserved)
Render button shows dirty after auto-save completes (render-dirty wiring preserved)
Reload from server resets save status to saved (reloadFromServer behavior preserved)
git diff shows only moved code — no logic changes, no added/removed behavior

unresolved_flags.concern: Over-engineering: the `useTimelineSave` size target is not realistic for this repository as written. Step 5 still bundles almost all of the heavy logic from `useTimelineData` into one hook, so the executor will likely either miss the `<200 LOC` success criterion or introduce extra helper files that contradict the "6 extracted hooks only" constraint.
unresolved_flags.evidence: The code assigned to Step 5 is most of the original file: save/edit functions begin at [useTimelineData.ts](/Users/peteromalley/Documents/reigh-workspace/reigh-app/src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineData.ts#L205), poll/registry sync effects begin at [useTimelineData.ts](/Users/peteromalley/Documents/reigh-workspace/reigh-app/src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineData.ts#L392), and `reloadFromServer` begins at [useTimelineData.ts](/Users/peteromalley/Documents/reigh-workspace/reigh-app/src/tools/video-editor/hooks/useTimelineData.ts#L600). Even the plan's fallback only moves two helpers out, but Step 5 still retains well over 200 lines of logic, and that fallback also conflicts with the success criterion "No new files beyond the 6 extracted hooks".
